title: Peripheral Neuropathy — Approach
oneLiner: A length-dependent or patchy nerve dysfunction causing sensory, motor, or autonomic symptoms – common, often missed, and treatable if cause is found early.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
- **Classic vignette:** A 55-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (poorly controlled) presents with a 6-month history of "pins and needles" starting in his toes, now ascending to his ankles. He says it feels like walking on cotton wool. On examination, you find reduced vibration sense at the ankles, absent ankle jerks, and loss of pain sensation in a stocking distribution. His feet are dry and cracked. No weakness yet.

- **Dissect the vignette:**
    - *"Pins and needles"* → positive sensory symptom (dysesthesia) from small fibre involvement.
    - *Ascending from toes* → length-dependent (dying-back) pattern – longest axons hit first.
    - *"Walking on cotton wool"* → proprioceptive loss (large fibre) – impaired balance, high fall risk.
    - *Reduced vibration sense at ankles* → large fibre dorsal column dysfunction.
    - *Absent ankle jerks* → loss of deep tendon reflex – afferent limb (spindle or nerve) or efferent alpha motor axon; here length-dependent.
    - *Stocking distribution* → classic “glove-and-stocking” pattern – small fibres.
    - *Dry cracked feet* → autonomic involvement (sympathetic denervation – loss of sweating leads to xerosis).
    - *No weakness yet* → predominantly sensory; motor involvement happens later in chronic axonal neuropathies.

- **Atypical presentations (may trick you):**
    1. **Mononeuritis multiplex** – sudden foot drop or wrist drop, then another nerve days/weeks later (think vasculitic – e.g., systemic lupus erythematosus, polyarteritis nodosa, or diabetes). Pattern is patchy, not length-dependent.
    2. **Acute ascending paralysis** – Guillain-Barré syndrome (AIDP): rapid onset (days to weeks), ascending weakness initially without sensory loss, areflexia early, often following infection or vaccination. Can mimic spinal cord lesion if reflexes are lost only after a few days.
    3. **Small fibre neuropathy alone** – burning pain, allodynia, but normal strength, normal reflexes, normal nerve conduction studies (NCS). Often missed because NCS is normal – think diabetes, Fabry disease, or idiopathic.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
- **Plain-language definition:** A group of disorders where peripheral nerves (beyond the spinal roots) are damaged, causing disordered sensation, muscle weakness, and/or autonomic dysfunction. It can be genetic, acquired, or due to systemic disease.

- **Why it matters:**
    - **Frequency:** Very common – up to 2-3% of general population, >50% in long-standing diabetes.
    - **Stakes:** Untreated causes can lead to permanent weakness (e.g., CIDP reversible, but delayed treatment = axonal loss), foot ulcers, falls, fractures, and arrhythmia from autonomic involvement.
    - **Common pitfalls:**
        - Mistaking peripheral neuropathy for radiculopathy (which is dermatomal, not length-dependent).
        - Missing treatable causes like B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, or paraproteinemia.
        - Relying on normal nerve conduction studies to rule out neuropathy – small fibre neuropathy can have normal NCS/EMG.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
- **The story:** Peripheral nerves are long, metabolically vulnerable neurons. Damage can occur at the myelin sheath (demyelinating – slows conduction) or the axon (axonal – reduces amplitude). The longest axons (to feet) require most energy, so damage often appears distally first – “dying-back” pattern.

- **Why each key sign happens:**
    - *Reduced vibration sense* – large myelinated fibres (IA) mediating proprioception/vibration are the fastest conducting; demyelination or axonal loss impairs them early in many neuropathies.
    - *Absent ankle jerk* – distortion of the monosynaptic reflex arc – both afferent (vibration) and efferent (motor) fibres are affected; ankle is the longest stretch reflex arc.
    - *Stocking-glove sensory loss* – small unmyelinated C-fibres (pain, temperature) have no myelin sheath but are length-dependent; they degenerate as farthest from cell body.
    - *Dry skin* – sympathetic vasoconstrictor and sudomotor fibres (unmyelinated) travel with nerves; loss of sweat gland innervation causes anhidrosis and skin breakdown.
    - *Pain* – small fibre involvement; damaged C-fibres fire spontaneously or become sensitised (peripheral sensitisation) causing burning or stabbing pain.

- **Connection to pattern cues:**
    - Length-dependent symmetric (stocking before glove) suggests metabolic, toxic, or hereditary causes.
    - Asymmetric or patchy suggests vasculitis, multifocal (e.g., leprosy, hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies).
    - Acute (days to weeks) suggests inflammatory demyelinating process (AIDP/GBS), vasculitis, or toxic (thallium, arsenic).
    - Chronic (months to years) suggests diabetes, B12 deficiency, uremia, hereditary or idiopathic.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Metabolic/Endocrine** | Diabetes mellitus (most common overall) | Long-standing, poor glycemic control; often painless or painful sensorimotor; may present with foot ulcer |
| | Uremia (chronic kidney disease) | BUN/creatinine >5x normal; symmetric; may improve with dialysis |
| | Hypothyroidism | Slow reflexes (hung-up), cold intolerance, weight gain, fatigue |
| **Nutritional** | B12 deficiency | Macrocytic anaemia, glossitis, subacute combined degeneration (sensory ataxia + spasticity), often vegans or malabsorption |
| | Thiamine deficiency (alcoholic, malnourished) | Can present with acute sensorimotor plus Wernicke’s encephalopathy (confusion, ophthalmoplegia) |
| | Pyridoxine excess (B6) | History of megadose supplement (in gym, or isoniazid without pyridoxine) |
| **Toxic** | Alcohol | Chronic heavy drinking, symmetric, painful, often with amblyopia or cerebellar signs |
| | Chemotherapy (vincristine, cisplatin, taxanes) | Acute/subacute during or after treatment; dose-dependent |
| | Heavy metals (lead, arsenic, thallium) | Lead: wrist drop + abdominal pain + blue line; arsenic: rice water diarrhea, Mee’s lines; thallium: alopecia, painful neuropathy |
| **Inflammatory/Dysimmune** | Guillain-Barré (AIDP) | Rapid ascending paralysis (over days), areflexia, CSF albumin-cytologic dissociation, post-infection (Campylobacter, CMV) |
| | CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy) | Symmetric proximal AND distal weakness, relapsing-remitting, elevated CSF protein |
| | Vasculitic (mononeuritis multiplex) | Asymmetric, painful, serial mononeuropathies; + systemic signs (rash, arthritis, glomerulonephritis) |
| | Sarcoidosis | Cranial nerve (especially VII) + peripheral?, skin/chest/hilar lymphadenopathy |
| **Paraproteinemic** | MGUS (monoclonal gammopathy) | Older patient, symmetric, distal, often sensory only; screen with serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) + immunofixation |
| | Amyloidosis (AL type) | Carpal tunnel syndrome + autonomic (orthostasis, diarrhoea, erectile dysfunction) + nephrotic range proteinuria |
| **Hereditary** | Charcot-Marie-Tooth (CMT type 1 – demyelinating) | Slowly progressive foot drop, high arches (pes cavus), hammer toes, areflexia, onset <20 years; family history |
| | Hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies (HNPP) | Recurrent mononeuropathies at compression sites (fibular, ulnar), mild demyelination on NCS |
| **Infectious** | HIV | Paresthesia early (distal symmetrical polyneuropathy) or mononeuritis multiplex later; associated with antiretroviral therapy |
| | Leprosy (Hansen disease) | Painless skin patches with anaesthesia, thickened nerves (especially ulnar, greater auricular), areas with geographic endemicity |
| | Lyme disease (Borreliosis) | Cranial neuritis (Bilateral facial palsy), radiculopathy, erythema migrans |
| **Idiopathic** | “Cryptogenic” sensory neuropathy | After extensive workup, no cause found; usually older, benign course; monitor for progression and treat with symptom management |

**Most common cause worldwide:** Distal symmetric polyneuropathy due to **diabetes mellitus**. Always check HbA1c and random glucose first.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic approach:** Stepwise – confirm neuropathy, determine pattern (axonal vs demyelinating), search for cause.

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Fingerstick glucose & HbA1c** | Random glucose, glycaemic control | Rule out diabetes (most common cause) | HbA1c ≥6.5% = diabetes; even if normal, consider impaired glucose tolerance |
| **Full blood count (FBC)** | MCV, RBC indices, Hb, WBC | B12 deficiency (macrocytic), myelodysplasia, infection | Macrocytosis → check B12/folate |
| **B12, folate** | Vitamin levels | Vitamin deficiency neuropathy | B12 <150 pmol/L (or elevated MMA/homocysteine) |
| **Thyroid function (TSH, free T4)** | Thyroid gland function | Hypothyroid neuropathy | Elevated TSH = hypothyroidism |
| **Renal function (BUN/Cr)** | Kidney function | Uremic neuropathy | GFR <30 often causes |
| **Serum protein electrophoresis + immunofixation** | Monoclonal gammopathy (M-spike) | Paraproteinemic neuropathy (MGUS, amyloid, myeloma) | If positive, further workup: serum free light chains, urine Bence-Jones, bone marrow biopsy if myeloma suspected |
| **Antinuclear antibody (ANA), ESR, CRP** | Systemic inflammation | Vasculitic neuropathy (mononeuritis multiplex) | High titres → rheumatology input; consider anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA) |
| **HIV, Lyme serology** | Infections | If risk factors or features (mononeuritis, facial palsy) | Positive → treat accordingly |
| **Nerve conduction studies (NCS) + EMG** | Conduction velocities, amplitudes, F waves, H reflex | Distinguish axonal vs demyelinating, pattern (symmetric, multifocal), neuromuscular junction | Demyelinating: slow CV, prolonged distal latencies, conduction block; Axonal: low amplitude, normal or mild slowing |
| **Lumbar puncture** | CSF protein, cell count, glucose, oligoclonal bands | Suspicion of inflammatory polyneuropathy (GBS, CIDP, sarcoid) | **GBS:** elevated protein, normal WBC (albumin-cytologic dissociation); **CIDP:** high protein ± oligoclonal bands; **infectious (Lyme):** pleocytosis |
| **Nerve biopsy** (rarely needed) | Histology: amyloid, vasculitis, inflammation | If diagnosis unclear, especially with atypical findings | Use sural nerve biopsy (sensory); risk of neuropathic pain at donor site |

**Pattern-recognition singular finding:**
- **Confirmed demyelination on NCS** → narrows to GBS, CIDP, CMT1 (familial), paraproteinemic, or leprosy.
- **Mononeuritis multiplex + vasculitic markers** → treat with high-dose steroids + immunosuppression; urgent rheumatology.
- **Normal NCS but burning pain + allodynia** → small fibre neuropathy – consider skin biopsy for intraepidermal nerve fibre density (confirm).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning: **Key principle:** Treat the underlying cause first – reversal or halting progression. Symptomatic management for pain and functional deficits is essential.
sections.Immediate (first hour):
- **Address acute causes:**
    - Suspected **Guillain-Barré syndrome** (rapid ascending weakness, areflexia) → admit to neurology, monitor respiratory function (FVC, NIF) – 30% require ICU. No specific medication in first hour; prepare for IVIG or plasma exchange.
    - **Acute vasculitic mononeuritis multiplex** (new/worsening asymmetric weakness over days) → high-dose steroids IV (methylprednisolone 1g IV for 3-5 days) as first line, plus cyclophosphamide if severe.
    - **Toxic neuropathy** from heavy metals → chelation (depending on metal) but only if acute exposure; for chronic alcohol – thiamine IV.
- **ABC: if respiratory muscles involved (GBS, critical illness polyneuropathy)** → check forced vital capacity (FVC) – if <15 mL/kg, or rapid decline, plan for ICU admission and non-invasive ventilation/intubation.
sections.Ward (24-72 hours):
- **Identify and treat underlying cause (48-72h workup window):**
    - **Diabetes:** optimise glycemic control (insulin or oral agents, target HbA1c <7% if safe)
    - **B12 deficiency:** IM hydroxocobalamin 1000mcg every other day for 2 weeks, then monthly
    - **Hypothyroidism:** levothyroxine 1.6mcg/kg PO daily (start low in older patients)
    - **Uremia:** initiate dialysis; neuropathy may partially improve
    - **Paraproteinemia / amyloid:** haematology consultation; consider chemotherapy (bortezomib + dexamethasone for AL amyloid)
- **Neuropathic pain management (start within 24h if pain is moderate-severe):**
    - First-line: **gabapentin** 300 mg PO nocte, titrate up by 300 mg every 3 days to effective dose (typical 900-1800 mg/day in divided doses) or **pregabalin** 75 mg PO bd, increase to 150-300 mg bd as tolerated.
    - Alternative: **Amitriptyline** 10-25 mg PO nocte (start low, increase by 10-25 mg weekly, max 150 mg) – anticholinergic side effects, avoid in elderly/glaucoma/prostate.
    - **Duloxetine** 30-60 mg PO daily (especially diabetic neuropathy) – more evidence than other SNRIs.
    - **Topical lidocaine 5% patch** for focal neuropathic pain (non-inferior to oral, fewer systemic effects).
- **Physical therapy and orthotics:**
    - Assess foot care (podiatry), ankle-foot orthosis if foot drop; gait training.
- **Educate on foot care:** daily inspection, moisturise (no cracks), well-fitting shoes, avoid walking barefoot.
sections.Long-term (outpatient):
- **Revisit cause if initial workup negative** → consider genetic testing (CMT, HNPP), amyloid fat pad biopsy, nerve biopsy if progressive.
- **Titrate symptomatic medications** to maximum tolerated dose; consider combination therapy (e.g., gabapentin + amitriptyline) if monotherapy fails.
- **Monitor progression** with serial NCS (every 6-12 months) and clinical scales (e.g., Modified Rankin, Neuropathy Impairment Score).
- **Refer to Pain Clinic** if refractory neuropathic pain; consider spinal cord stimulation in selected patients (especially failed back surgery syndrome+neuropathy).
- **Consider TENS (transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation)** – non-pharmacologic, low risk, some benefit.
- **For CIDP/GBS:** immunosuppressants (steroids, azathioprine, mycophenolate) if chronic; IVIG or plasma exchange as periodic therapy.
sections.What NOT to do (common pitfalls):
- **Do NOT prescribe opioids** as first-line for neuropathic pain – limited efficacy, risk of dependence, constipation, and no disease modification.
- **Do NOT start steroids without ruling out infection** (especially for mononeuritis) – can flare tuberculosis, hepatitis B.
- **Do NOT use NSAIDs** – no evidence, renal risk in older patients.
- **Do NOT put the patient on bedrest** – encourage mobility to prevent deconditioning, contractures, DVT.
- **Do NOT rely on normal NCS to exclude neuropathy** – small fibre neuropathy is NCS-silent; need clinical diagnosis ± skin biopsy.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
The top mimics of peripheral neuropathy (as a set of symptoms) are:

| Feature | Peripheral Neuropathy (DSP) | Radiculopathy (lumbar/sacral) | Myasthenia Gravis | Motor Neuron Disease (ALS) |
|---------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain** | Yes, burning/stabbing/tingling (positive sensory) | Radicular pain (shooting, dermatomal) | No pain (fatigue) | Painless fasciculations, cramps |
| **Sensory** | Stocking-glove loss (pain, temperature, vibration) | Dermatomal sensory loss (e.g., L4-L5-S1) | None | None (except mild secondary sensory at late stage) |
| **Motor** | Distal weakness (late, symmetric), foot drop | Myotomal weakness (L5 foot drop, S1 plantar flexion) | Fluctuating proximal weakness (fatiguable – worse with use, better with rest) | Asymmetric distal/proximal weakness with fasciculations, spasticity (UMN signs) |
| **Reflexes** | Reduced/absent, especially ankle jerks (length-dependent) | Reduced reflex in affected myotome | Preserved or hyperreflexia in myasthenia? (usually normal) | Hyperreflexia (UMN), but can lose them as LMN degenerates |
| **ECG/EMG/NCS** | NCS – slow CV or low amplitude; EMG – denervation potentials | NCS normal (unless severe) – needle EMG shows denervation in myotome | SFEMG – increased jitter; repetitive stimulation – decrement | EMG – diffuse active denervation (fasciculations, PSW, sharp waves), normal NCV |
| **Labs** | Check glucose, B12, TSH, SPEP, ANA | Usually normal; MRI shows disc/stenosis | AChR antibodies positive (80% ocular, 90% generalized) | Normal AChR, may have anti-GM1 or anti-GD1a (if lower motor neuron variant) |
| **Key diff** | Length-dependent sensory loss is hallmark | Radicular pain pattern & dermatomal sensory loss is localising | Fatiguable weakness, no sensory loss, ocular/bulbar involvement | Combined UMN and LMN signs, no sensory loss, relentless progression |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What To Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Foot ulcers and osteomyelitis** | Painless wound on weight-bearing area of foot (e.g., metatarsal head, heel); often missed because patient does not feel pain. | Cellulitis, purulent discharge, exposed bone, probe-to-bone positive; X-ray changes (periosteal reaction, bone destruction) | Urgent podiatry/orthopaedics, wound debridement, culture, ABX (empiric: co-amoxiclav or piperacillin-tazobactam if severe), offloading, and check for osteomyelitis (MRI) |
| **Charcot neuroarthropathy** | Sudden, painless swelling, warmth, and deformity of foot/ankle. Misdiagnosed as cellulitis or gout if pain is present due to inflammation. | Midfoot collapse (“rocker bottom”) or ankle deformity; X-ray shows fragmentation, disorganisation, and obliteration of joint space | IMMEDIATE immobilisation (total contact cast) and offloading to prevent deformity; orthotics; surgical reconstruction if failed conservative; treat diabetes |
| **Falls and fractures** | Unexplained falls, hip fracture, wrist fracture. | Poor gait (wide-base, high stepping) due to proprioceptive loss; impaired ankle dorsiflexion (foot drop) | Physiotherapy: balance training, ankle-foot orthosis, strength training; educate patient on fall risk and home modifications; treat underlying cause |
| **Autonomic neuropathy** (especially diabetes) | Orthostatic hypotension, gastroparesis, erectile dysfunction, arrhythmia (QT prolongation) | BP drop on standing (>20 mmHg systolic), resting tachycardia, abnormal heart rate variability, gastroparesis on gastric emptying study | Management: fludrocortisone/midodrine for OH, metoclopramide for gastroparesis, sildenafil for ED, beta-blockers for QT; monitor with 24h ECG; high risk of sudden death |
| **Diaphragmatic/Respiratory failure** (GBS, CIDP, critical illness) | Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, reduced cough, fatigue; rapid progression may be fatal | Reduced FVC (<15-20 mL/kg), Tiffeneau-Pinelli sign (paradoxical breathing), use of accessory muscles, desaturation | Immediate ICU admission, NIPPV/intubation, monitor NIF/FVC q4h; start IVIG or plasma exchange (GBS) |
| **Motor disability & foot drop** | Chronic foot drop (unable to dorsiflex), causing tripping and falls; weak toe extension | Ankle dorsiflexion <5/5 MRC, high-stepping gait | AFO (ankle-foot orthosis) immediately; consider functional electrical stimulation (FES) for walking; surgical transfer (tibialis posterior) if no recovery after 12 months |

**Late-presentation pattern (the stem that tests “what happens if untreated”):**
- A diabetic patient with neuropathy who developed a painless foot ulcer → untreated → osteomyelitis → amputation.
- A patient with undiagnosed GBS → progressive weakness → respiratory failure requiring ventilation → prolonged ICU stay → critical illness polyneuropathy/myopathy.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
- **Mnemonic for common causes of symmetric distal polyneuropathy:** **“D-A-B” or “D-A-B-L-E-D”** (the common treatable ones):
    - **D** – Diabetes
    - **A** – Alcohol
    - **B** – B12 deficiency
    - **L** – Low thyroid (hypothyroidism)
    - **E** – Excess B6 (pyridoxine)
    - **D** – Drugs (chemotherapy, isoniazid, metronidazole)

- **Mnemonic for patterns of neuropathy:**  
    **“M-S-P”**
    - **M** – Mononeuritis multiplex (multi-focal)
    - **S** – Symmetric distal polyneuropathy
    - **P** – Proximal > distal myopathy (but usually not neuropathy) – think CIDP or porphyria

- **Pearl 1:** Always check a **random glucose and HbA1c** in every new neuropathy – if normal, consider **impaired glucose tolerance** (2h OGTT) in painful sensory neuropathy.
- **Pearl 2:** In patients >60 with symmetric sensory neuropathy and **normal routine workup**, think **paraproteinemia (MGUS)** – order serum protein electrophoresis and immunofixation.
- **Pearl 3:** **Mononeuritis multiplex** until proven otherwise is **vasculitic** – do not wait for nerve biopsy; start high-dose steroids early, then taper.
- **Pearl 4:** The combination of **facial nerve palsy + lower limb radiculopathy** in a patient from a **Lyme-endemic area** is pathognomonic for **neuroborreliosis**.
- **Pearl 5:** **Guillain-Barré** typically presents with **areflexia** – if you give an MRI of spine showing enhancement of nerve roots, that’s classic. Remember to monitor **FVC** (not just O2 saturations).
- **Pearl 6:** **Electromyography/nerve conduction studies** cannot assess small fibres – if clinical suspicion of small fibre neuropathy, consider **skin biopsy** (intraepidermal nerve fibre density) or **QSART** (quantitative sudomotor axon reflex test).

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Mistaking **proprioceptive loss** for **ataxia** (it is sensory ataxia).
- Starting **gabapentin** 300mg tid on day 1 → dizzy, falls, sedation. Always **start low, go slow**.
- Not checking **B12 before starting folates** in anaemia – can precipitate subacute combined degeneration.
- Using **amitriptyline** in elderly patients with prostatic hypertrophy or glaucoma.
- Ordering a **nerve biopsy** without cause – high morbidity; only when diagnosis remains uncertain and treatable cause suspected (e.g., amyloid, vasculitis, sarcoid).
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call for senior review (neurology registrar or consultant):**

| Criteria | Threshold | Action |
|----------|-----------|--------|
| **Rapidly progressive weakness (< 2 weeks)** | Any weakness affecting legs & arms; or bulbar (slurred speech, swallowing) | Urgent neurology review; suspect GBS/CIDP, vasculitis |
| **Respiratory decline** | FVC <20 mL/kg or falling >5% per hour; NIF <30 cmH2O; pCO2 >6.0 kPa | Crash call/ICU; prepare for NIPPV/intubation |
| **Autonomic instability** | Heart rate instability (alternating brady/tachy), postural hypotension (sustained BP drop >20 mmHg despite fluids), arrhythmia (QTc >500 ms) | Call

rawMarkdown:
# Peripheral Neuropathy — Approach
**One-liner:** A length-dependent or patchy nerve dysfunction causing sensory, motor, or autonomic symptoms – common, often missed, and treatable if cause is found early.

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
- **Classic vignette:** A 55-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (poorly controlled) presents with a 6-month history of "pins and needles" starting in his toes, now ascending to his ankles. He says it feels like walking on cotton wool. On examination, you find reduced vibration sense at the ankles, absent ankle jerks, and loss of pain sensation in a stocking distribution. His feet are dry and cracked. No weakness yet.

- **Dissect the vignette:**
    - *"Pins and needles"* → positive sensory symptom (dysesthesia) from small fibre involvement.
    - *Ascending from toes* → length-dependent (dying-back) pattern – longest axons hit first.
    - *"Walking on cotton wool"* → proprioceptive loss (large fibre) – impaired balance, high fall risk.
    - *Reduced vibration sense at ankles* → large fibre dorsal column dysfunction.
    - *Absent ankle jerks* → loss of deep tendon reflex – afferent limb (spindle or nerve) or efferent alpha motor axon; here length-dependent.
    - *Stocking distribution* → classic “glove-and-stocking” pattern – small fibres.
    - *Dry cracked feet* → autonomic involvement (sympathetic denervation – loss of sweating leads to xerosis).
    - *No weakness yet* → predominantly sensory; motor involvement happens later in chronic axonal neuropathies.

- **Atypical presentations (may trick you):**
    1. **Mononeuritis multiplex** – sudden foot drop or wrist drop, then another nerve days/weeks later (think vasculitic – e.g., systemic lupus erythematosus, polyarteritis nodosa, or diabetes). Pattern is patchy, not length-dependent.
    2. **Acute ascending paralysis** – Guillain-Barré syndrome (AIDP): rapid onset (days to weeks), ascending weakness initially without sensory loss, areflexia early, often following infection or vaccination. Can mimic spinal cord lesion if reflexes are lost only after a few days.
    3. **Small fibre neuropathy alone** – burning pain, allodynia, but normal strength, normal reflexes, normal nerve conduction studies (NCS). Often missed because NCS is normal – think diabetes, Fabry disease, or idiopathic.

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
- **Plain-language definition:** A group of disorders where peripheral nerves (beyond the spinal roots) are damaged, causing disordered sensation, muscle weakness, and/or autonomic dysfunction. It can be genetic, acquired, or due to systemic disease.

- **Why it matters:**
    - **Frequency:** Very common – up to 2-3% of general population, >50% in long-standing diabetes.
    - **Stakes:** Untreated causes can lead to permanent weakness (e.g., CIDP reversible, but delayed treatment = axonal loss), foot ulcers, falls, fractures, and arrhythmia from autonomic involvement.
    - **Common pitfalls:**
        - Mistaking peripheral neuropathy for radiculopathy (which is dermatomal, not length-dependent).
        - Missing treatable causes like B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, or paraproteinemia.
        - Relying on normal nerve conduction studies to rule out neuropathy – small fibre neuropathy can have normal NCS/EMG.

## 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
- **The story:** Peripheral nerves are long, metabolically vulnerable neurons. Damage can occur at the myelin sheath (demyelinating – slows conduction) or the axon (axonal – reduces amplitude). The longest axons (to feet) require most energy, so damage often appears distally first – “dying-back” pattern.

- **Why each key sign happens:**
    - *Reduced vibration sense* – large myelinated fibres (IA) mediating proprioception/vibration are the fastest conducting; demyelination or axonal loss impairs them early in many neuropathies.
    - *Absent ankle jerk* – distortion of the monosynaptic reflex arc – both afferent (vibration) and efferent (motor) fibres are affected; ankle is the longest stretch reflex arc.
    - *Stocking-glove sensory loss* – small unmyelinated C-fibres (pain, temperature) have no myelin sheath but are length-dependent; they degenerate as farthest from cell body.
    - *Dry skin* – sympathetic vasoconstrictor and sudomotor fibres (unmyelinated) travel with nerves; loss of sweat gland innervation causes anhidrosis and skin breakdown.
    - *Pain* – small fibre involvement; damaged C-fibres fire spontaneously or become sensitised (peripheral sensitisation) causing burning or stabbing pain.

- **Connection to pattern cues:**
    - Length-dependent symmetric (stocking before glove) suggests metabolic, toxic, or hereditary causes.
    - Asymmetric or patchy suggests vasculitis, multifocal (e.g., leprosy, hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies).
    - Acute (days to weeks) suggests inflammatory demyelinating process (AIDP/GBS), vasculitis, or toxic (thallium, arsenic).
    - Chronic (months to years) suggests diabetes, B12 deficiency, uremia, hereditary or idiopathic.

## 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Metabolic/Endocrine** | Diabetes mellitus (most common overall) | Long-standing, poor glycemic control; often painless or painful sensorimotor; may present with foot ulcer |
| | Uremia (chronic kidney disease) | BUN/creatinine >5x normal; symmetric; may improve with dialysis |
| | Hypothyroidism | Slow reflexes (hung-up), cold intolerance, weight gain, fatigue |
| **Nutritional** | B12 deficiency | Macrocytic anaemia, glossitis, subacute combined degeneration (sensory ataxia + spasticity), often vegans or malabsorption |
| | Thiamine deficiency (alcoholic, malnourished) | Can present with acute sensorimotor plus Wernicke’s encephalopathy (confusion, ophthalmoplegia) |
| | Pyridoxine excess (B6) | History of megadose supplement (in gym, or isoniazid without pyridoxine) |
| **Toxic** | Alcohol | Chronic heavy drinking, symmetric, painful, often with amblyopia or cerebellar signs |
| | Chemotherapy (vincristine, cisplatin, taxanes) | Acute/subacute during or after treatment; dose-dependent |
| | Heavy metals (lead, arsenic, thallium) | Lead: wrist drop + abdominal pain + blue line; arsenic: rice water diarrhea, Mee’s lines; thallium: alopecia, painful neuropathy |
| **Inflammatory/Dysimmune** | Guillain-Barré (AIDP) | Rapid ascending paralysis (over days), areflexia, CSF albumin-cytologic dissociation, post-infection (Campylobacter, CMV) |
| | CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy) | Symmetric proximal AND distal weakness, relapsing-remitting, elevated CSF protein |
| | Vasculitic (mononeuritis multiplex) | Asymmetric, painful, serial mononeuropathies; + systemic signs (rash, arthritis, glomerulonephritis) |
| | Sarcoidosis | Cranial nerve (especially VII) + peripheral?, skin/chest/hilar lymphadenopathy |
| **Paraproteinemic** | MGUS (monoclonal gammopathy) | Older patient, symmetric, distal, often sensory only; screen with serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) + immunofixation |
| | Amyloidosis (AL type) | Carpal tunnel syndrome + autonomic (orthostasis, diarrhoea, erectile dysfunction) + nephrotic range proteinuria |
| **Hereditary** | Charcot-Marie-Tooth (CMT type 1 – demyelinating) | Slowly progressive foot drop, high arches (pes cavus), hammer toes, areflexia, onset <20 years; family history |
| | Hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies (HNPP) | Recurrent mononeuropathies at compression sites (fibular, ulnar), mild demyelination on NCS |
| **Infectious** | HIV | Paresthesia early (distal symmetrical polyneuropathy) or mononeuritis multiplex later; associated with antiretroviral therapy |
| | Leprosy (Hansen disease) | Painless skin patches with anaesthesia, thickened nerves (especially ulnar, greater auricular), areas with geographic endemicity |
| | Lyme disease (Borreliosis) | Cranial neuritis (Bilateral facial palsy), radiculopathy, erythema migrans |
| **Idiopathic** | “Cryptogenic” sensory neuropathy | After extensive workup, no cause found; usually older, benign course; monitor for progression and treat with symptom management |

**Most common cause worldwide:** Distal symmetric polyneuropathy due to **diabetes mellitus**. Always check HbA1c and random glucose first.

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic approach:** Stepwise – confirm neuropathy, determine pattern (axonal vs demyelinating), search for cause.

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Fingerstick glucose & HbA1c** | Random glucose, glycaemic control | Rule out diabetes (most common cause) | HbA1c ≥6.5% = diabetes; even if normal, consider impaired glucose tolerance |
| **Full blood count (FBC)** | MCV, RBC indices, Hb, WBC | B12 deficiency (macrocytic), myelodysplasia, infection | Macrocytosis → check B12/folate |
| **B12, folate** | Vitamin levels | Vitamin deficiency neuropathy | B12 <150 pmol/L (or elevated MMA/homocysteine) |
| **Thyroid function (TSH, free T4)** | Thyroid gland function | Hypothyroid neuropathy | Elevated TSH = hypothyroidism |
| **Renal function (BUN/Cr)** | Kidney function | Uremic neuropathy | GFR <30 often causes |
| **Serum protein electrophoresis + immunofixation** | Monoclonal gammopathy (M-spike) | Paraproteinemic neuropathy (MGUS, amyloid, myeloma) | If positive, further workup: serum free light chains, urine Bence-Jones, bone marrow biopsy if myeloma suspected |
| **Antinuclear antibody (ANA), ESR, CRP** | Systemic inflammation | Vasculitic neuropathy (mononeuritis multiplex) | High titres → rheumatology input; consider anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA) |
| **HIV, Lyme serology** | Infections | If risk factors or features (mononeuritis, facial palsy) | Positive → treat accordingly |
| **Nerve conduction studies (NCS) + EMG** | Conduction velocities, amplitudes, F waves, H reflex | Distinguish axonal vs demyelinating, pattern (symmetric, multifocal), neuromuscular junction | Demyelinating: slow CV, prolonged distal latencies, conduction block; Axonal: low amplitude, normal or mild slowing |
| **Lumbar puncture** | CSF protein, cell count, glucose, oligoclonal bands | Suspicion of inflammatory polyneuropathy (GBS, CIDP, sarcoid) | **GBS:** elevated protein, normal WBC (albumin-cytologic dissociation); **CIDP:** high protein ± oligoclonal bands; **infectious (Lyme):** pleocytosis |
| **Nerve biopsy** (rarely needed) | Histology: amyloid, vasculitis, inflammation | If diagnosis unclear, especially with atypical findings | Use sural nerve biopsy (sensory); risk of neuropathic pain at donor site |

**Pattern-recognition singular finding:**
- **Confirmed demyelination on NCS** → narrows to GBS, CIDP, CMT1 (familial), paraproteinemic, or leprosy.
- **Mononeuritis multiplex + vasculitic markers** → treat with high-dose steroids + immunosuppression; urgent rheumatology.
- **Normal NCS but burning pain + allodynia** → small fibre neuropathy – consider skin biopsy for intraepidermal nerve fibre density (confirm).

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Key principle:** Treat the underlying cause first – reversal or halting progression. Symptomatic management for pain and functional deficits is essential.

### Immediate (first hour)
- **Address acute causes:**
    - Suspected **Guillain-Barré syndrome** (rapid ascending weakness, areflexia) → admit to neurology, monitor respiratory function (FVC, NIF) – 30% require ICU. No specific medication in first hour; prepare for IVIG or plasma exchange.
    - **Acute vasculitic mononeuritis multiplex** (new/worsening asymmetric weakness over days) → high-dose steroids IV (methylprednisolone 1g IV for 3-5 days) as first line, plus cyclophosphamide if severe.
    - **Toxic neuropathy** from heavy metals → chelation (depending on metal) but only if acute exposure; for chronic alcohol – thiamine IV.
- **ABC: if respiratory muscles involved (GBS, critical illness polyneuropathy)** → check forced vital capacity (FVC) – if <15 mL/kg, or rapid decline, plan for ICU admission and non-invasive ventilation/intubation.

### Ward (24-72 hours)
- **Identify and treat underlying cause (48-72h workup window):**
    - **Diabetes:** optimise glycemic control (insulin or oral agents, target HbA1c <7% if safe)
    - **B12 deficiency:** IM hydroxocobalamin 1000mcg every other day for 2 weeks, then monthly
    - **Hypothyroidism:** levothyroxine 1.6mcg/kg PO daily (start low in older patients)
    - **Uremia:** initiate dialysis; neuropathy may partially improve
    - **Paraproteinemia / amyloid:** haematology consultation; consider chemotherapy (bortezomib + dexamethasone for AL amyloid)
- **Neuropathic pain management (start within 24h if pain is moderate-severe):**
    - First-line: **gabapentin** 300 mg PO nocte, titrate up by 300 mg every 3 days to effective dose (typical 900-1800 mg/day in divided doses) or **pregabalin** 75 mg PO bd, increase to 150-300 mg bd as tolerated.
    - Alternative: **Amitriptyline** 10-25 mg PO nocte (start low, increase by 10-25 mg weekly, max 150 mg) – anticholinergic side effects, avoid in elderly/glaucoma/prostate.
    - **Duloxetine** 30-60 mg PO daily (especially diabetic neuropathy) – more evidence than other SNRIs.
    - **Topical lidocaine 5% patch** for focal neuropathic pain (non-inferior to oral, fewer systemic effects).
- **Physical therapy and orthotics:**
    - Assess foot care (podiatry), ankle-foot orthosis if foot drop; gait training.
- **Educate on foot care:** daily inspection, moisturise (no cracks), well-fitting shoes, avoid walking barefoot.

### Long-term (outpatient)
- **Revisit cause if initial workup negative** → consider genetic testing (CMT, HNPP), amyloid fat pad biopsy, nerve biopsy if progressive.
- **Titrate symptomatic medications** to maximum tolerated dose; consider combination therapy (e.g., gabapentin + amitriptyline) if monotherapy fails.
- **Monitor progression** with serial NCS (every 6-12 months) and clinical scales (e.g., Modified Rankin, Neuropathy Impairment Score).
- **Refer to Pain Clinic** if refractory neuropathic pain; consider spinal cord stimulation in selected patients (especially failed back surgery syndrome+neuropathy).
- **Consider TENS (transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation)** – non-pharmacologic, low risk, some benefit.
- **For CIDP/GBS:** immunosuppressants (steroids, azathioprine, mycophenolate) if chronic; IVIG or plasma exchange as periodic therapy.

### What NOT to do (common pitfalls)
- **Do NOT prescribe opioids** as first-line for neuropathic pain – limited efficacy, risk of dependence, constipation, and no disease modification.
- **Do NOT start steroids without ruling out infection** (especially for mononeuritis) – can flare tuberculosis, hepatitis B.
- **Do NOT use NSAIDs** – no evidence, renal risk in older patients.
- **Do NOT put the patient on bedrest** – encourage mobility to prevent deconditioning, contractures, DVT.
- **Do NOT rely on normal NCS to exclude neuropathy** – small fibre neuropathy is NCS-silent; need clinical diagnosis ± skin biopsy.

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
The top mimics of peripheral neuropathy (as a set of symptoms) are:

| Feature | Peripheral Neuropathy (DSP) | Radiculopathy (lumbar/sacral) | Myasthenia Gravis | Motor Neuron Disease (ALS) |
|---------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain** | Yes, burning/stabbing/tingling (positive sensory) | Radicular pain (shooting, dermatomal) | No pain (fatigue) | Painless fasciculations, cramps |
| **Sensory** | Stocking-glove loss (pain, temperature, vibration) | Dermatomal sensory loss (e.g., L4-L5-S1) | None | None (except mild secondary sensory at late stage) |
| **Motor** | Distal weakness (late, symmetric), foot drop | Myotomal weakness (L5 foot drop, S1 plantar flexion) | Fluctuating proximal weakness (fatiguable – worse with use, better with rest) | Asymmetric distal/proximal weakness with fasciculations, spasticity (UMN signs) |
| **Reflexes** | Reduced/absent, especially ankle jerks (length-dependent) | Reduced reflex in affected myotome | Preserved or hyperreflexia in myasthenia? (usually normal) | Hyperreflexia (UMN), but can lose them as LMN degenerates |
| **ECG/EMG/NCS** | NCS – slow CV or low amplitude; EMG – denervation potentials | NCS normal (unless severe) – needle EMG shows denervation in myotome | SFEMG – increased jitter; repetitive stimulation – decrement | EMG – diffuse active denervation (fasciculations, PSW, sharp waves), normal NCV |
| **Labs** | Check glucose, B12, TSH, SPEP, ANA | Usually normal; MRI shows disc/stenosis | AChR antibodies positive (80% ocular, 90% generalized) | Normal AChR, may have anti-GM1 or anti-GD1a (if lower motor neuron variant) |
| **Key diff** | Length-dependent sensory loss is hallmark | Radicular pain pattern & dermatomal sensory loss is localising | Fatiguable weakness, no sensory loss, ocular/bulbar involvement | Combined UMN and LMN signs, no sensory loss, relentless progression |

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What To Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Foot ulcers and osteomyelitis** | Painless wound on weight-bearing area of foot (e.g., metatarsal head, heel); often missed because patient does not feel pain. | Cellulitis, purulent discharge, exposed bone, probe-to-bone positive; X-ray changes (periosteal reaction, bone destruction) | Urgent podiatry/orthopaedics, wound debridement, culture, ABX (empiric: co-amoxiclav or piperacillin-tazobactam if severe), offloading, and check for osteomyelitis (MRI) |
| **Charcot neuroarthropathy** | Sudden, painless swelling, warmth, and deformity of foot/ankle. Misdiagnosed as cellulitis or gout if pain is present due to inflammation. | Midfoot collapse (“rocker bottom”) or ankle deformity; X-ray shows fragmentation, disorganisation, and obliteration of joint space | IMMEDIATE immobilisation (total contact cast) and offloading to prevent deformity; orthotics; surgical reconstruction if failed conservative; treat diabetes |
| **Falls and fractures** | Unexplained falls, hip fracture, wrist fracture. | Poor gait (wide-base, high stepping) due to proprioceptive loss; impaired ankle dorsiflexion (foot drop) | Physiotherapy: balance training, ankle-foot orthosis, strength training; educate patient on fall risk and home modifications; treat underlying cause |
| **Autonomic neuropathy** (especially diabetes) | Orthostatic hypotension, gastroparesis, erectile dysfunction, arrhythmia (QT prolongation) | BP drop on standing (>20 mmHg systolic), resting tachycardia, abnormal heart rate variability, gastroparesis on gastric emptying study | Management: fludrocortisone/midodrine for OH, metoclopramide for gastroparesis, sildenafil for ED, beta-blockers for QT; monitor with 24h ECG; high risk of sudden death |
| **Diaphragmatic/Respiratory failure** (GBS, CIDP, critical illness) | Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, reduced cough, fatigue; rapid progression may be fatal | Reduced FVC (<15-20 mL/kg), Tiffeneau-Pinelli sign (paradoxical breathing), use of accessory muscles, desaturation | Immediate ICU admission, NIPPV/intubation, monitor NIF/FVC q4h; start IVIG or plasma exchange (GBS) |
| **Motor disability & foot drop** | Chronic foot drop (unable to dorsiflex), causing tripping and falls; weak toe extension | Ankle dorsiflexion <5/5 MRC, high-stepping gait | AFO (ankle-foot orthosis) immediately; consider functional electrical stimulation (FES) for walking; surgical transfer (tibialis posterior) if no recovery after 12 months |

**Late-presentation pattern (the stem that tests “what happens if untreated”):**
- A diabetic patient with neuropathy who developed a painless foot ulcer → untreated → osteomyelitis → amputation.
- A patient with undiagnosed GBS → progressive weakness → respiratory failure requiring ventilation → prolonged ICU stay → critical illness polyneuropathy/myopathy.

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
- **Mnemonic for common causes of symmetric distal polyneuropathy:** **“D-A-B” or “D-A-B-L-E-D”** (the common treatable ones):
    - **D** – Diabetes
    - **A** – Alcohol
    - **B** – B12 deficiency
    - **L** – Low thyroid (hypothyroidism)
    - **E** – Excess B6 (pyridoxine)
    - **D** – Drugs (chemotherapy, isoniazid, metronidazole)

- **Mnemonic for patterns of neuropathy:**  
    **“M-S-P”**
    - **M** – Mononeuritis multiplex (multi-focal)
    - **S** – Symmetric distal polyneuropathy
    - **P** – Proximal > distal myopathy (but usually not neuropathy) – think CIDP or porphyria

- **Pearl 1:** Always check a **random glucose and HbA1c** in every new neuropathy – if normal, consider **impaired glucose tolerance** (2h OGTT) in painful sensory neuropathy.
- **Pearl 2:** In patients >60 with symmetric sensory neuropathy and **normal routine workup**, think **paraproteinemia (MGUS)** – order serum protein electrophoresis and immunofixation.
- **Pearl 3:** **Mononeuritis multiplex** until proven otherwise is **vasculitic** – do not wait for nerve biopsy; start high-dose steroids early, then taper.
- **Pearl 4:** The combination of **facial nerve palsy + lower limb radiculopathy** in a patient from a **Lyme-endemic area** is pathognomonic for **neuroborreliosis**.
- **Pearl 5:** **Guillain-Barré** typically presents with **areflexia** – if you give an MRI of spine showing enhancement of nerve roots, that’s classic. Remember to monitor **FVC** (not just O2 saturations).
- **Pearl 6:** **Electromyography/nerve conduction studies** cannot assess small fibres – if clinical suspicion of small fibre neuropathy, consider **skin biopsy** (intraepidermal nerve fibre density) or **QSART** (quantitative sudomotor axon reflex test).

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Mistaking **proprioceptive loss** for **ataxia** (it is sensory ataxia).
- Starting **gabapentin** 300mg tid on day 1 → dizzy, falls, sedation. Always **start low, go slow**.
- Not checking **B12 before starting folates** in anaemia – can precipitate subacute combined degeneration.
- Using **amitriptyline** in elderly patients with prostatic hypertrophy or glaucoma.
- Ordering a **nerve biopsy** without cause – high morbidity; only when diagnosis remains uncertain and treatable cause suspected (e.g., amyloid, vasculitis, sarcoid).

## 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call for senior review (neurology registrar or consultant):**

| Criteria | Threshold | Action |
|----------|-----------|--------|
| **Rapidly progressive weakness (< 2 weeks)** | Any weakness affecting legs & arms; or bulbar (slurred speech, swallowing) | Urgent neurology review; suspect GBS/CIDP, vasculitis |
| **Respiratory decline** | FVC <20 mL/kg or falling >5% per hour; NIF <30 cmH2O; pCO2 >6.0 kPa | Crash call/ICU; prepare for NIPPV/intubation |
| **Autonomic instability** | Heart rate instability (alternating brady/tachy), postural hypotension (sustained BP drop >20 mmHg despite fluids), arrhythmia (QTc >500 ms) | Call
